content.text:
You are performing the FINAL review of the codebase, focused on a single dimension: UI/UX (ui-ux).

Review ALL changes made during this workflow through the lens of this dimension only, and report your findings. The complete diff of the changes is provided below.

## Changes made during this workflow
(No git diff was available — the working directory may not be a git repository, or there are no uncommitted changes against HEAD. Review the working tree directly.)

OUTPUT RULES:
- If this review dimension is NOT applicable to the changeset (for example: a UI/UX review when there are no UI-facing changes, or a security review when there is no security-relevant surface), set applicable=false, explain in notApplicableReason, and return an empty findings array.
- Otherwise set applicable=true and list every real finding, ordered by severity (critical first).
- Rate each finding: low (nit / cosmetic), medium (should fix), high (important), critical (must fix before merge).
- For every finding, write a complete `fixPrompt` that a fixer agent can execute directly and in isolation to resolve it: state the file(s), the exact problem, and the intended fix. Do not reference other findings.
- If there are genuinely no issues for this dimension, return applicable=true with an empty findings array — NEVER fabricate findings.
- Set the `dimension` field of your response to exactly "ui-ux".

## Response Format (required)
Respond with ONLY a single valid JSON object matching the schema below.
- No prose, explanations, or commentary before or after the JSON.
- No markdown code fences (```), no headings — output the raw JSON object.
- Do NOT call any tools and do NOT end your turn on a thinking block. Emit the JSON object directly as text.

JSON schema:
{ dimension: The review dimension you assessed (must match the dimension assigned in the prompt), applicable: false when this review dimension does not apply to the changeset (e.g. a UI/UX review with no UI changes). When false, set notApplicableReason and return an empty findings array., notApplicableReason: Required when applicable=false. Explain why this dimension is irrelevant to the current changes. Empty string when applicable=true., summary: Overall assessment for this dimension (what you checked and your conclusion), findings: Array<{ id: Short stable kebab-case identifier, unique within this review (e.g. 'n-plus-one-user-query'), severity: "low" | "medium" | "high" | "critical" /* low = nit/cosmetic, medium = should fix, high = important, critical = must fix before merge */, file: Primary file the finding relates to, with line range if helpful (e.g. 'src/auth.ts:42-58'), title: Concise one-line summary of the finding, description: Detailed explanation: what is wrong, why it matters, and the intended correct behavior, fixPrompt: A complete, self-contained prompt that a fixer agent can execute directly to resolve this finding. Must state the file(s), the exact problem, and the intended fix. Do not reference other findings. }> /* Findings ordered by severity, most severe first. Empty when applicable=false or when there are genuinely no issues. */ }

content.thinking:
This is a significant change to a file browser application. Let me look at the actual UI code changes. The key files are:
- src/app.ts (modified - reduced significantly)
- src/app.css (modified)
- src/app/* (new modules)

Let me look at the full diff of the UI files. Let me start with app.ts, app.css, and the new modules.

Interesting. So the CSS diff shows that some styles were REMOVED but not replaced. Let me look at the full app.css to see the current state, and understand the classes that are actually used. The app.ts uses:
- `.file-browser-host`
- `.trigger` (button)
- `.browser-dialog`
- `.dialog-header`
- `.title`
- `.close-btn`
- `.dialog-body`
- `.file-browser`
- `.toolbar`
- `.breadcrumb`
- `.btn`
- `.results`
- `.status`

But the CSS diff shows the removal of `.breadcrumb .separator`, `.toolbar label.upload-btn`, and `.results-table .folder-row td`. These styles were removed but the classes might still be in use. Let me look at the full app.css and the dom-builders.ts to understand what's happening.

Wait — the upload label now uses class `btn` instead of `upload-btn`. And the breadcrumb separator and folder-row styles were removed. Let me check the dom-builders to see what classes are used now.

Now let me look at the dom-builders.ts to understand what elements are being built, and check for the removed classes. The CSS removed:
- `.breadcrumb .separator` (color: #3c3c3c; margin: 0 2px)
- `.toolbar label.upload-btn` (and its hover/input child rules)
- `.results-table .folder-row td` (font-weight: 600)

The app.ts now uses `uploadLabel.className = 'btn'` instead of `upload-btn`. And the `.btn` class is smaller (padding 4px 10px, font-size 12px) vs the old upload-btn (padding 6px 12px, font-size 13px). This creates a visual inconsistency where the Search button and Upload button would be different sizes if... wait, no. The Search button uses class `btn` too. So now Search and Upload both use `.btn`. That's actually consistent now.

But wait, let me check if `.breadcrumb .separator` and `.results-table .folder-row` are still used in the dom-builders. If they're still referenced, the removal would cause visual regressions.

Let me look at dom-builders.ts and the render files.

Now I see something. In `renderBreadcrumb`, the separator is created as a plain span with textContent '/' but NO class. The old CSS had `.breadcrumb .separator` styles (color #3c3c3c, margin: 0 2px). Since the separator no longer has the `separator` class, those styles wouldn't apply anyway. But now the separator is just plain text '/' with default color (#e6e6e6 from body) and no spacing. Wait, but `.breadcrumb` has `gap: 4px` so there's spacing. But the color is now #e6e6e6 (light) instead of #3c3c3c (dark). That's a minor visual change.

Actually wait — let me check the separator. The breadcrumb has `gap: 4px` so the separators get 4px gap on each side. The separators would render in the body color #e6e6e6. The links are #9a9a9a. So separators would actually be brighter than the links, which is a bit odd but minor.

Let me focus on the bigger UI/UX issues. Let me look at the render-orchestrator and render-search and toolbar-handlers.

Now let me understand the full picture. Let me look at the original (pre-change) app.ts to understand what was there before. Let me look at the git diff of app.ts and the removed test files.

Key observations so far for UI/UX:

1. **Search input type**: In `app.ts`, `searchInput.type = 'text'` instead of `'search'`. The CSS handles both `.toolbar input[type="search"]` and `.toolbar input[type="text"]`. This is fine functionally but `type="search"` provides a native clear button and semantics. Minor.

2. **Upload label**: Now uses `class="btn"` — but the label still has a hidden file input inside. The `.btn` has `white-space: nowrap` and is sized smaller. This works visually as a button. But there's a concern: a `<label>` with class `btn` — is it focusable/keyboard accessible? A label wrapping an input is focusable via the input. The hidden input (`hidden = true`) — when an input is hidden via the `hidden` attribute, it removes it from the layout AND from the accessibility tree (display:none essentially). So the upload input is NOT keyboard accessible! This is an accessibility concern. Wait — but the old CSS used `display: none` on the input too. Let me think.

Actually, `uploadInput.hidden = true` sets the `hidden` HTML attribute which applies `display: none`. A `display:none` input cannot receive focus, so keyboard users cannot trigger the upload via the label's wrapping. Actually, a `<label>` that wraps a focusable input — clicking the label focuses the input, but if the input is `display:none`, then... Actually clicking the label still triggers a click on the input (which opens the file dialog) even if the input is display:none. But keyboard focus: the input is removed from the tab order when display:none. So keyboard users have NO way to reach the upload control. This is a real accessibility issue — but was it introduced by this change? The old code used `display: none` in CSS on the input. So this might be a pre-existing issue.

Wait, let me re-read. The CSS diff shows the OLD `.toolbar label.upload-btn input[type="file"] { display: none; }` was removed. And the NEW code uses `uploadInput.hidden = true`. Both achieve display:none. So this accessibility issue is carried over, not newly introduced. But since this is a review of "all changes made during this workflow," and the upload button was rebuilt (app.ts was heavily modified), it's worth flagging. Actually, the original app.ts — let me check what it looked like. Let me look at the original.

Actually, let me focus on what's genuinely NEW or CHANGED. Let me look at the original app.ts and app.css to compare.

So the original app.ts already had `searchInput.type = 'text'`, `uploadInput.hidden = true`, `uploadLabel.className = 'btn'`. So the upload-label-as-btn pattern and the hidden input were already there before this workflow. These are pre-existing patterns, NOT introduced by this workflow. 

But wait — the task says "Review ALL changes made during this workflow." The refactor restructured everything into modules but preserved the same DOM structure and behavior. So the UI output should be essentially identical to before. The CSS changes removed unused styles (`.breadcrumb .separator`, `.toolbar label.upload-btn`, `.results-table .folder-row td`).

Let me check whether `.breadcrumb .separator` and `.results-table .folder-row` classes are still being emitted in the new dom-builders. From dom-builders.ts:
- `renderBreadcrumb` creates separators as plain `span` with NO class (just `separator.textContent = '/'`). So `.breadcrumb .separator` selector no longer matches — that's fine, the rule was correctly removed.
- `makeBrowseRow` — the row is `const row = document.createElement('tr')` with no className. So `.folder-row` class is NOT applied. The old `makeBrowseRow` must have added `folder-row` class. Let me verify by checking the original.

So the CSS removal of `.results-table .folder-row td { font-weight: 600; }` corresponds to the removal of the `folder-row` class on folder rows. Let me check the original to confirm folder rows no longer get bold styling. Actually, in the new dom-builders, folder rows don't get any distinguishing style (no bold). This is a minor visual regression — folders no longer visually distinguished from files by font weight. But it's minor.

Wait — actually, let me check the original dom-builders / app.ts to see if folder-row was used.

This is very informative. The original `app.ts` at the branch point (6f55a97) ALREADY had:
- `searchInput.type = 'text'`
- `uploadInput.hidden = true`
- `uploadLabel.className = 'btn'`
- breadcrumb separators as plain span (no class)
- `makeBrowseRow` with NO `folder-row` class
- `makeActionButton` WITHOUT try/catch error handling

So the CSS rules `.breadcrumb .separator`, `.toolbar label.upload-btn`, `.results-table .folder-row td` were ALREADY dead code (not matching any elements) at the branch point. The removal in app.css is just cleaning up already-dead CSS. That's NOT a regression introduced by this workflow.

Now what did THIS workflow actually change in terms of UI/UX behavior?

1. **`makeActionButton` now has try/catch error handling** — surfaces errors in the status footer. This is an IMPROVEMENT for UX (errors now show feedback instead of silently failing). Good.

2. **`handleUpload` now collects per-file failures** — surfaces "Uploaded N file(s); failed: ..." in status. This is an IMPROVEMENT (was: any failure would throw and show generic error; now partial success is handled). Good.

3. **render-orchestrator `render()` now has a render-race guard (renderToken)** — IMPROVEMENT.

4. **`init` tears down previous listener** — fixes memory leak. Not UI.

5. The DOM structure is identical.

So actually, this workflow is primarily a refactor (splitting app.ts into modules) + bug hardening (error handling, race guards). The rendered DOM is byte-for-byte equivalent in structure.

Let me reconsider whether there are genuinely any UI/UX REGRESSIONS or issues. Since the DOM output is essentially identical to before, and the changes are improvements to error handling/feedback, the UI/UX review would mostly be: "improvements, no regressions."

But wait — I should still review the CURRENT state of the UI for real UI/UX issues, even if pre-existing. The instructions say "Review ALL changes made during this workflow." But the refactor touches all the UI code, so effectively the whole UI is "changed" (moved). However, the guidance also says not to fabricate findings, and to focus on what changed.

Let me think about what's genuinely worth flagging. Let me reconsider the whole UI from a UX lens, focusing on things that this refactor might have affected or that are clearly present in the current code:

**Accessibility concerns present in the code (some pre-existing, but the code was rewritten in this workflow):**

1. **Upload control not keyboard accessible**: `uploadInput.hidden = true` makes the file input `display:none`, removing it from the tab order. A keyboard-only user cannot reach/activate the Upload control. The `<label class="btn">` is not focusable on its own (labels aren't focusable unless they wrap a focusable, reachable control). This is a real a11y issue. It was present before, but the code was rewritten in this workflow (handleUpload moved to toolbar-handlers.ts, the DOM building preserved in app.ts). Hmm, is this in scope? The task is reviewing changes. The pattern was preserved identically. I'd consider this borderline. Given it's a genuine, important a11y defect and the code was touched/rewritten, I think it's worth flagging as a finding — but I should note it. Actually, let me reconsider: the instructions emphasize reviewing changes. Since the upload DOM construction was moved but kept identical, the defect is pre-existing. But many reviewers would flag it. Let me think about severity — it's a genuine keyboard a11y blocker.

Actually, let me reconsider. The standard pattern for a "styled-as-button file upload" is to visually hide the input with a technique that keeps it focusable (e.g., `position:absolute; opacity:0; clip` or visually-hidden/sr-only), NOT `display:none`. With `display:none`, the label's click still works (mouse), but keyboard users are locked out. This is a legitimate finding. I'll include it as medium/high.

2. **Search input uses `type="text"` instead of `type="search"`**: Loses native search semantics (clear button, "search" role hint to assistive tech, Enter-to-submit affordance is handled manually so that's OK). Minor. The CSS even has rules for `input[type="search"]` which are now dead-ish (both are styled identically). Minor/low.

3. **No `<label>` association for the search input**: The search input has a `placeholder="Search..."` but no associated `<label>`. Placeholders disappear on input and aren't a substitute for an accessible name. Screen reader users won't get a clear label. This is an a11y issue. Pre-existing but code rewritten. Medium.

4. **Close button uses an icon font (`<i class="bi bi-x-lg">`) with `aria-label="Close"`** — that's fine actually. Good — the aria-label is set.

5. **`<dialog>` focus management**: `dialog.showModal()` handles focus trapping natively. Good. The close button has aria-label. Good.

6. **Error/success feedback**: Now action button errors surface in status footer (improvement). But the error message format `'Error: ' + err.message` could be more user-friendly. Minor.

7. **Loading state**: There's NO loading indicator. When `render()` fetches, it clears the results and shows nothing until data arrives. On slow connections, the user sees a blank results area with no "Loading..." indicator. This is a real UX gap (perceived performance & feedback). Pre-existing but the render lifecycle was rewritten (render-orchestrator.ts is new). I think this is worth flagging as medium — the orchestrator explicitly clears results synchronously then awaits with no loading state.

8. **Empty state**: When a folder has no entries, the table shows just headers with no "This folder is empty" message. Minor UX gap. Pre-existing.

9. **Delete confirmation**: Uses `window.confirm` — fine, accessible.

10. **Move/Copy use `window.prompt`** — functional but jarring native prompt. Pre-existing. Low/medium UX polish.

Let me focus on the most defensible, real findings that relate to the changes. The render lifecycle rewrite is genuinely new code (render-orchestrator.ts), so the missing loading state is directly tied to new code. The action button error handling and upload error handling are genuinely new behaviors. The DOM builders are moved but structurally identical.

Let me be careful not to fabricate or over-flag. Let me reconsider each:

- **Loading state (render-orchestrator.ts)**: The new `render()` does `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` then `await api.browse(...)`. During the await, the results area is empty with no loading indicator. This is a genuine perceived-performance/feedback gap introduced/embodied by the new orchestrator. The status footer isn't cleared either (intentionally, per comments), so it might show stale status. This is a legit medium finding. Actually, the status is intentionally not cleared to preserve navigation targets — so during a pending fetch, the OLD folder's breadcrumb + status remain visible while the table is wiped. That's actually a somewhat decent "keep context" approach, but the empty table with no spinner is still a gap. Medium.

- **Search input missing accessible label**: Genuine a11y issue. The search input relies on placeholder only. Medium.

- **Upload not keyboard accessible**: Genuine a11y blocker. `hidden` input → not focusable. This is the most serious. But is it "changed"? The DOM construction is in app.ts which WAS modified (369 lines reduced, restructured). The specific upload block is identical though. I'll flag it as high because it's a real accessibility blocker and the code is part of the changeset (rewritten). Actually, I want to be careful. Let me re-read the diff stat: `src/app.ts | 369 +-------` — that's a big reduction (logic moved out). The upload DOM construction stayed in app.ts. Let me check if the upload block in app.ts changed at all between 6f55a97 and HEAD.

From the original (6f55a97) app.ts:
```
const uploadLabel = document.createElement('label');
uploadLabel.className = 'btn';
uploadLabel.textContent = 'Upload';
uploadInput = document.createElement('input');
uploadInput.type = 'file';
uploadInput.hidden = true;
uploadInput.multiple = true;
uploadLabel.append(uploadInput);
```

From current app.ts:
```
const uploadLabel = document.createElement('label');
uploadLabel.className = 'btn';
uploadLabel.textContent = 'Upload';
const uploadInput = document.createElement('input');
uploadInput.type = 'file';
uploadInput.hidden = true;
uploadInput.multiple = true;
uploadLabel.append(uploadInput);
```

Identical (except `const` scoping since refs moved to context). So the upload DOM construction is byte-identical. This defect is purely pre-existing and not touched. Per the instructions ("Review ALL changes made during this workflow"), and to avoid fabricating findings on unchanged code, I should be cautious. However, the instruction also says the review should focus on the changes. A pre-existing defect that's literally untouched isn't a "change."

Hmm. Let me reconsider the scope. The honest answer: this workflow is a refactor + hardening. The rendered UI is structurally identical. The genuinely NEW UX behaviors are:
1. Action button errors now surface in status footer (improvement — `makeActionButton` try/catch).
2. Upload partial-failure handling (improvement — `handleUpload` failed list).
3. Render-race guard (improvement).

These are all improvements. There are no UI/UX regressions introduced.

But are there any genuine issues in the NEW code specifically? Let me look closely at the new error handling for potential UX issues:

`makeActionButton`:
```js
btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
  try {
    Promise.resolve(handler()).catch((err) => {
      getStatus().textContent = 'Error: ' + ...;
    });
  } catch (err) {
    getStatus().textContent = 'Error: ' + ...;
  }
});
```
This shows errors but provides no success feedback. For Move/Copy/Delete success, there's no confirmation message (just a silent re-render). Minor — but the table updates so it's visible. OK.

`handleUpload`: shows `Uploaded N file(s); failed: ...` on partial failure. But on FULL success, it shows nothing (status is set by rerender to the folder count). That's fine.

Now, one thing: when `handleUpload` succeeds fully, `rerender()` sets status to "N folders, M files, total S". There's no "Upload complete" confirmation. Minor — the file appearing in the list is feedback enough.

Let me reconsider the loading state more carefully because render-orchestrator.ts is genuinely new code. Is the lack of a loading state a regression vs. the original? The original `render()` also did `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` then awaited with no loading indicator. So it's identical behavior — not a regression, just a pre-existing gap embodied in rewritten code.

OK here's my honest assessment: This is a refactor + hardening. The UI output is structurally identical to before. The new behaviors (error surfacing, partial-upload handling, race guard) are strict UX improvements. There are NO new UI/UX regressions.

However, there ARE pre-existing UI/UX issues present in the code that was rewritten/moved. The question is whether to flag them. This is genuinely in new code. The orchestrator's design (clear sync, await, then render) is a direct choice in the new file. **Defensible — medium.** Actually I want to separate: (a) loading indicator missing, (b) empty state missing. Both relate to render/render-browse.

2. **Search input lacks accessible label** (app.ts — the input has only placeholder): This is a11y. The input construction is in app.ts (modified file). **Defensible — medium.** Though it's identical to before... but app.ts is in the diff.

3. **Upload control not keyboard-accessible** (app.ts — `hidden` input): a11y blocker. In app.ts. **Defensible — high**, though pre-existing pattern.

Hmm, I'm torn on whether to flag #2 and #3 since they're literally unchanged. Let me lean towards the spirit: the reviewer should report real UI/UX issues in the changeset. The changeset includes app.ts (modified), and the rendered UI. Actually, you know what — I'll be measured. Let me flag:
- The loading/empty state gap (genuinely in new orchestrator + render modules) — medium.
- The search input accessible label — medium (it's in the modified app.ts).
- The upload keyboard inaccessibility — high (genuinely important a11y, in modified app.ts).

But wait, I should double check the "hidden" input claim. `uploadInput.hidden = true`. The `hidden` global attribute maps to `display: none` per HTML spec. A `display:none` form control is not rendered and not focusable. The label wraps it. Per HTML spec, clicking a `<label>` that contains its associated control fires a click on the control (this works even for display:none inputs → opens file picker for mouse users). But for keyboard: the label itself is not focusable (labels aren't interactive elements and don't receive keyboard focus). The wrapped input is display:none → not in tab order. So keyboard users have no way to focus or activate the upload. This is a real a11y failure. The fix is to use a visually-hidden technique (sr-only / position:absolute + clip) instead of `hidden`, OR make the label a button-like focusable element. So this is a legit high/critical a11y finding.

Is it critical or high? Per severity guidance: "critical = UI is broken / unusable / inaccessible to a whole group of users (e.g. keyboard users cannot complete the flow)." Keyboard users cannot use the Upload control. That meets the "critical" bar arguably. But it's a pre-existing issue and upload is arguably a secondary feature (browsing/downloading works via keyboard). I'll rate it high to be safe (it's important, not necessarily blocking the primary flow). Actually the guidance says keyboard users cannot complete THE FLOW = critical. The upload flow specifically is inaccessible to keyboard users. I'll go high since it's one of several flows, not the entire app.

Hmm, but again — it's unchanged code. Let me reconsider whether to include at all. I think I'll include it but frame it accurately. Actually, let me reconsider the whole thing once more.

Given the instruction "If there are genuinely no issues for this dimension, return applicable=true with an empty findings array — NEVER fabricate findings." And the strong emphasis on not fabricating. The safest honest answer considers: is this a UI/UX change? YES (it's a file browser UI, app.ts/css/dom-builders/render modules all modified). Let me settle on flagging:

And maybe the empty state as low. Let me also double-check: is there a focus issue when the dialog opens (focus management)? `showModal()` moves focus to the first focusable in the dialog or the dialog itself. That's fine. When closed, focus returns to the trigger button (native dialog behavior). Good.

Actually, wait. Let me reconsider one more potential issue: the `<dialog>` is in the DOM but the widget lives in the embedded host. The trigger button is placed AFTER the embedded host in the DOM (root.append(embeddedHost, trigger, dialog)). So visually, the big browser widget is at top, then the "Browse Files" trigger button below it, then the (hidden) dialog. That's a reasonable layout: the embedded browser is the primary surface, and the button opens the dialog version. OK.

But is there a concern that when the dialog is open AND the widget is moved into it, the embedded host becomes empty (just an empty flex container taking up space)? No — when the dialog is modal open, the backdrop covers the page, so the empty host is hidden behind the backdrop. Fine.

Let me also check: the `trigger` button text is "Browse Files" but the browser is ALREADY visible (embedded). So the button's purpose might be confusing — "Browse Files" when files are already browsable. It opens a dialog version. Minor microcopy clarity issue. Low. I'll consider mentioning but it's minor.

Let me also verify the app.css removal of `.breadcrumb .separator` doesn't break the separator styling. The separators are now plain '/' in body color with the flex gap. Previously the rule was dead anyway (no class). So no regression. Fine.

Now, let me reconsider the severity and whether I'm being fair. Let me also verify there isn't a broken focus state or contrast issue. The `.btn` and links have hover states but I don't see explicit `:focus-visible` styles. Focus outlines — the browser default focus ring should appear. There's `outline: none` on the search input (`.toolbar input[type="text"] { outline: none; }`)! That removes the focus outline and replaces it only with a border-color change (`:focus { border-color: #6aa6ff; }`). The border-color change IS a visible focus indicator (though subtle — border goes from #3c3c3c to #6aa6ff). That's acceptable but borderline for WCAG (focus indicator visibility). For buttons (.btn), there's no `:focus` style at all, so they rely on the browser default focus ring. That's fine. 

The `outline: none` on the search input with only a border-color change is a minor a11y concern (focus indicator could be more prominent), but the border-color change does provide a visible focus state. Low/medium. I'll consider mentioning but it might be too nitpicky.

Let me also check: color contrast. The status text is #9a9a9a on #1e1e1e. Contrast ratio of #9a9a9a (RGB 154,154,154) on #1e1e1e (RGB 30,30,30): luminance... #9a9a9a is mid-gray. Contrast ratio ≈ 5.3:1 roughly. That passes WCAG AA for normal text (4.5:1). OK fine. The breadcrumb links #9a9a9a same. OK.

Yes — clears, awaits, then renders. No "Loading..." text or spinner shown during the await. On first load this means a blank results panel. Confirmed. And the status footer is intentionally NOT cleared (shows stale "N folders..." from before, or empty on first load). So on first load: empty table, empty status, no loading indicator. Real gap.

Now let me write the findings. I need complete fixPrompts.

For the upload a11y fix:
File: src/app.ts (upload input construction) and src/app.css.
Problem: `uploadInput.hidden = true` makes the input `display:none`, removing it from tab order and making it unfocusable. Keyboard users cannot activate Upload.
Fix: Don't use `hidden`. Instead keep the input focusable but visually hidden. Options:
- Remove `uploadInput.hidden = true` and instead visually hide it with CSS that preserves focusability, e.g., a `.visually-hidden` / sr-only class: `position:absolute; width:1px; height:1px; padding:0; margin:-1px; overflow:hidden; clip:rect(0,0,0,0); white-space:nowrap; border:0;`. Add that class to the input. Also add `aria-label="Upload files"`. Keep the label association (label wraps input).
- Ensure the label/input shows a visible focus ring when focused.

For search label:
File: src/app.ts (search input construction).
Problem: Search input relies on placeholder only, no accessible name.
Fix: Add `searchInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Search files');` (or associate a visible label). Keep placeholder as hint.

For loading state:
File: src/app/render-orchestrator.ts.
Problem: render() clears results and awaits fetch with no loading indicator.
Fix: After clearing results, set a loading indicator, e.g., `resultsEl.textContent = 'Loading…';` (or a status message) before the await, then replace on success. Optionally set status. Clear it on success/error.

Actually wait — the status is intentionally not cleared. But adding a resultsEl "Loading..." would be fine and is cleared anyway. Let me suggest setting `resultsEl.textContent = 'Loading…';` right after the innerHTML clear, before the await.

For empty state:
File: src/app/render-browse.ts.
Problem: empty folder shows empty table with no message.
Fix: After appending entries, if result.entries is empty (and parent handling), display an empty-state row or message like "This folder is empty." Could insert a full-width row.

These are genuine UX improvements and the rendered DOM structure is identical to before (the `app.css` deletions only removed already-dead selectors). Reviewing the current UI surface, I found several real accessibility and interaction-feedback gaps in code within the changeset: the Upload control is unreachable for keyboard users, the search input lacks an accessible name, there is no loading indicator during async fetches, no empty state, and the secondary 'Browse Files' trigger has ambiguous microcopy. No critical breakage; ordered by severity below.","findings":[{"id":"upload-control-keyboard-inaccessible","severity":"high","file":"src/app.ts:78-86","title":"Upload control is unreachable via keyboard (input is display:none)","description":"The file input is created with `uploadInput.hidden = true`, which applies the HTML `hidden` attribute → UA `display:none`. A `display:none` form control is removed from the tab order and cannot receive focus. The visible 'Upload' affordance is a wrapping `<label class=\"btn\">`; labels themselves are not focusable/activatable via keyboard. Net effect: keyboard-only and screen-reader users have no way to focus or trigger the Upload control, while mouse users can (clicking the label forwards a click to the hidden input). This is a genuine WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) failure for the upload flow. The pattern was preserved through the refactor and lives in code touched by this changeset.","fixPrompt":"In `src/app.ts`, the upload input is built around the line `uploadInput.hidden = true;` inside `startApp` (the `const uploadInput = document.createElement('input'); uploadInput.type = 'file'; uploadInput.hidden = true; uploadInput.multiple = true;` block). Remove `uploadInput.hidden = true;` (do not set the `hidden` attribute). Instead, keep the input focusable but visually hidden: give it a class such as `visually-hidden` (e.g. `uploadInput.className = 'visually-hidden';`) and add `uploadInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Upload files');`. Then in `src/app.css` add a `.visually-hidden { position: absolute; width: 1px; height: 1px; padding: 0; margin: -1px; overflow: hidden; clip: rect(0, 0, 0, 0); white-space: nowrap; border: 0; }` rule (a standard sr-only pattern that preserves focusability). Because the `<label class=\"btn\">` still wraps the input, Tab will now move focus onto the (visually-hidden) input inside the visible 'Upload' button, and Enter/Space will open the file picker. Verify that a visible focus ring appears around the Upload button when focused (add a `:focus-within` style on `.toolbar label.btn` if the default outline does not show). Do not change any other behavior."},{"id":"search-input-missing-accessible-name","severity":"medium","file":"src/app.ts:69-71","title":"Search input relies only on a placeholder, no accessible name","description":"The search input is created with `searchInput.type = 'text'` and `searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...'` but no associated `<label>`, `aria-label`, or `title`. A placeholder is not an accessible name: it is not exposed reliably to assistive technology and disappears as soon as the user types, leaving the field unlabeled. Screen-reader users get no clear purpose for the field. The field construction is part of the modified `app.ts`.","fixPrompt":"In `src/app.ts`, in the `startApp` toolbar-building block where `const searchInput = document.createElement('input'); searchInput.type = 'text'; searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...';` is defined, add an accessible name immediately after, e.g. `searchInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Search files');`. Keep the existing `placeholder` as a hint. Alternatively, add a visible `<label>` element and associate it via `for`/`id`. Make no other changes; ensure existing tests (`src/app/render-search.test.ts`, `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts`) still query the input by its current selectors."},{"id":"no-loading-state-during-fetch","severity":"medium","file":"src/app/render-orchestrator.ts:73","title":"No loading indicator while results are being fetched","description":"In `render()`, the results container is cleared synchronously (`resultsEl.innerHTML = '';`) and then the route data is awaited. The status footer is intentionally left untouched (to preserve navigation targets), so on the first load the user sees an empty table and empty status with no indication that work is in progress. This hurts perceived performance and can look like a broken/empty UI, especially on slower connections or larger directories. This lifecycle is newly authored in `render-orchestrator.ts`.","fixPrompt":"In `src/app/render-orchestrator.ts`, inside the `render()` function, immediately after the line `resultsEl.innerHTML = '';` (and before the `try`/`await getApi().browse(...)` / `search(...)` calls), insert a synchronous loading placeholder into the results element, e.g. `resultsEl.textContent = 'Loading…';`. This text is naturally replaced on success by `renderBrowse(result)` / `renderSearch(result)` (which set `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` then append the table) and on error by the existing `resultsEl.textContent = 'Error: ' + ...` branch, so no additional cleanup is required. Keep the existing race-guard token logic and the intentional non-clearing of the breadcrumb/status intact."},{"id":"no-empty-state-for-empty-folders","severity":"low","file":"src/app/render-browse.ts:30-38","title":"Empty folders render a bare table with no empty-state message","description":"`renderBrowse()` builds the results table and appends rows; if `result.entries` is empty (and there is no parent '..' row), the table shows only the column headers with an empty body and no explanation. Consider clearer microcopy that communicates it opens a pop-out/dialog version.","fixPrompt":"In `src/app.ts`, locate the trigger button construction (`const trigger = document.createElement('button'); trigger.className = 'trigger'; trigger.textContent = 'Browse Files';`) and change `trigger.textContent` to a label that conveys it opens the dialog/pop-out view, e.g. 'Open in dialog' or 'Pop out browser'. content.thinking: The previous attempt failed because `notApplicableReason` is required even when `applicable=true`. I need to include `notApplicableReason` with an empty string. Let me re-emit the same findings with that fix.

content.text: {"dimension":"ui-ux","applicable":true,"notApplicableReason":"","summary":"This workflow restructured the file-browser SPA (split src/app.ts into focused src/app/* modules: render-orchestrator, render-browse, render-search, dom-builders, toolbar-handlers, context) and hardened behavior — action-button errors now surface in the status footer, uploads report per-file partial failures, and a render-race guard was added. These are genuine UX improvements and the rendered DOM structure is identical to before (the app.css deletions only removed already-dead selectors). Reviewing the current UI surface, I found several real accessibility and interaction-feedback gaps in code within the changeset: the Upload control is unreachable for keyboard users, the search input lacks an accessible name, there is no loading indicator during async fetches, no empty state, and the secondary 'Browse Files' trigger has ambiguous microcopy. No critical breakage; ordered by severity below.","findings":[{"id":"upload-control-keyboard-inaccessible","severity":"high","file":"src/app.ts:78-86","title":"Upload control is unreachable via keyboard (input is hidden via display:none)","description":"The file input is created with `uploadInput.hidden = true`, which applies the HTML `hidden` attribute → UA `display:none`. A `display:none` form control is removed from the tab order and cannot receive focus. The visible 'Upload' affordance is a wrapping `<label class=\"btn\">`; labels themselves are not focusable/activatable via keyboard. Net effect: keyboard-only and screen-reader users have no way to focus or trigger the Upload control, while mouse users can (clicking the label forwards a click to the hidden input). This is a genuine WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) failure for the upload flow. The pattern was preserved through the refactor and lives in code touched by this changeset.","fixPrompt":"In `src/app.ts`, the upload input is built around the line `uploadInput.hidden = true;` inside `startApp` (the `const uploadInput = document.createElement('input'); uploadInput.type = 'file'; uploadInput.hidden = true; uploadInput.multiple = true;` block). Remove `uploadInput.hidden = true;` (do not set the `hidden` attribute). Instead, keep the input focusable but visually hidden: give it a class such as `visually-hidden` (e.g. `uploadInput.className = 'visually-hidden';`) and add `uploadInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Upload files');`. Then in `src/app.css` add a `.visually-hidden { position: absolute; width: 1px; height: 1px; padding: 0; margin: -1px; overflow: hidden; clip: rect(0, 0, 0, 0); white-space: nowrap; border: 0; }` rule (a standard sr-only pattern that preserves focusability). Because the `<label class=\"btn\">` still wraps the input, Tab will now move focus onto the (visually-hidden) input inside the visible 'Upload' button, and Enter/Space will open the file picker. Verify that a visible focus ring appears around the Upload button when focused (add a `:focus-within` style on `.toolbar label.btn` if the default outline does not show). Do not change any other behavior."},{"id":"search-input-missing-accessible-name","severity":"medium","file":"src/app.ts:69-71","title":"Search input relies only on a placeholder, no accessible name","description":"The search input is created with `searchInput.type = 'text'` and `searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...'` but no associated `<label>`, `aria-label`, or `title`. A placeholder is not an accessible name: it is not exposed reliably to assistive technology and disappears as soon as the user types, leaving the field unlabeled. Screen-reader users get no clear purpose for the field. The field construction is part of the modified `app.ts`.","fixPrompt":"In `src/app.ts`, in the `startApp` toolbar-building block where `const searchInput = document.createElement('input'); searchInput.type = 'text'; searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...';` is defined, add an accessible name immediately after, e.g. `searchInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Search files');`. Keep the existing `placeholder` as a hint. Alternatively, add a visible `<label>` element and associate it via `for`/`id`. Make no other changes; ensure existing tests (`src/app/render-search.test.ts`, `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts`) still query the input by its current selectors."},{"id":"no-loading-state-during-fetch","severity":"medium","file":"src/app/render-orchestrator.ts:73","title":"No loading indicator while results are being fetched","description":"In `render()`, the results container is cleared synchronously (`resultsEl.innerHTML = '';`) and then the route data is awaited. During that await there is no loading, skeleton, or 'Loading…' state — the results panel is simply blank. The status footer is intentionally left untouched (to preserve navigation targets), so on the first load the user sees an empty table and empty status with no indication that work is in progress. This hurts perceived performance and can look like a broken/empty UI, especially on slower connections or larger directories. This lifecycle is newly authored in `render-orchestrator.ts`.","fixPrompt":"In `src/app/render-orchestrator.ts`, inside the `render()` function, immediately after the line `resultsEl.innerHTML = '';` (and before the `try`/`await getApi().browse(...)` / `search(...)` calls), insert a synchronous loading placeholder into the results element, e.g. `resultsEl.textContent = 'Loading…';`. This text is naturally replaced on success by `renderBrowse(result)` / `renderSearch(result)` (which set `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` then append the table) and on error by the existing `resultsEl.textContent = 'Error: ' + ...` branch, so no additional cleanup is required. Keep the existing race-guard token logic and the intentional non-clearing of the breadcrumb/status intact."},{"id":"no-empty-state-for-empty-folders","severity":"low","file":"src/app/render-browse.ts:30-38","title":"Empty folders render a bare table with no empty-state message","description":"`renderBrowse()` builds the results table and appends rows; if `result.entries` is empty (and there is no parent '..' row), the table shows only the column headers with an empty body and no explanation. Users perceive a blank/missing listing rather than an explicitly empty folder. `render-browse.ts` is newly authored in this workflow.","fixPrompt":"In `src/app/render-browse.ts`, in `renderBrowse()`, after the loop that appends rows (`for (const entry of result.entries) { tbody.append(makeBrowseRow(entry)); }`) and before `resultsEl.append(table);`, add a check: if `tbody.children.length === 0` (no parent row and no entries), append an empty-state row spanning all columns, e.g. create a `<tr>` with a single `<td colspan=\"4\">` whose `textContent` is 'This folder is empty.' and append it to `tbody`. Use `document.createElement` / `textContent` only (no innerHTML). Do not alter the status footer or breadcrumb logic."},{"id":"trigger-button-microcopy-ambiguous","severity":"low","file":"src/app.ts:103-105","title":"'Browse Files' trigger is ambiguous next to an already-visible browser","description":"The page renders a fully interactive embedded file browser as the primary surface, and directly below it a button labeled 'Browse Files'. Since files are already browsable inline, the button's purpose is unclear — a user may not realize it opens a second, dialog/modal view of the same browser. Consider clearer microcopy that communicates it opens a pop-out/dialog version.","fixPrompt":"In `src/app.ts`, locate the trigger button construction (`const trigger = document.createElement('button'); trigger.className = 'trigger'; trigger.textContent = 'Browse Files';`) and change `trigger.textContent` to a label that conveys it opens the dialog/pop-out view, e.g. 'Open in dialog' or 'Pop out browser'. Keep the class name and click handler unchanged. If a tooltip is desired, you may add `trigger.title = 'Open the file browser in a dialog window';`. No other behavior should change."}]}
